channel.title: RakuAI Blog
channel.description: Engineering notes from the RakuAI team.

item.title: Agents as Team, Not Agents as Tool
item.description: The dev loop with parallel AI agents working against the same codebase at the same time. The bottleneck is no longer typing. It is deciding which four problems are worth working on simultaneously, and which two of the four results are good enough to keep.

item.title: Experiences as Code, Not Experiences as Assets
item.description: The .raku file is a JSON document. That choice is load-bearing. Here is what a real one looks like, why we picked JSON over a custom DSL, and how the file format becomes the contract between the human author and the AI runtime.

item.title: AI as Interface, Not AI as Dependency
item.description: If AI is a runtime primitive in the engine, you might assume the engine is locked to whichever model we wired in. It is not. Here is how the AI layer is exposed through interfaces instead of imports, and why that decision keeps getting more valuable.

item.title: AI as Nervous System, Not AI as Factory
item.description: Most engines that say 'AI-native' in 2026 mean they bolted on a chat panel. RakuAI is built differently. AI is a runtime primitive, not a feature next to the engine.

item.title: Foundation Built BY Agents, Not Foundation Built FOR Agents
item.description: An autonomous agent shipped ten foundational engine subsystems in a single Saturday. The engine's foundation is being built BY agents, in a workflow that treats agent-authored diffs as a first-class part of the dev loop.

item.title: The Week the Engine Grew Up
item.description: A 276-commit week landed TensorFlow Lite for on-device SLM inference, AES-256-GCM federated sync, OpenXR composition layers, Kalman-filtered anchor stabilization, and a hardware-abstraction layer for the optical channel. The engine ends 2025 with the surface area of a real platform.

item.title: The Day Visual Studio 2026 Hated Our Code
item.description: The runtime built clean on Linux. The runtime built clean on macOS. Then I tried to build it in Visual Studio 2026 Insiders, and Visual Studio 2026 had opinions. Twelve PRs later, the build is green. Here is the inside of those twelve PRs.

item.title: Claude Writes, Gemini Reviews, ChatGPT Rubber-Ducks
item.description: Three months into building this engine, the dev workflow has settled into a multi-vendor rhythm. Different models do different parts of the loop. Here is how I divide the work and why I think the multi-vendor approach is the right one for serious AI-native engineering.

item.title: OpenXR Is the Skeleton, Dual-Link Radios Are the Nerve
item.description: This week the runtime grew its OpenXR backbone (graphics binding, frame lifecycle, action sync) and a dual-mode RF / optical link manager that lets the tether switch radios on the fly. Both decisions are bets on standards. Both bets are working.

item.title: Built to Take Direction from ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini
item.description: This week the runtime got an XRAssistantService that integrates cloud LLM responses into the voice pipeline, plus OpenXR foundations, eye-tracked foveated rendering, full-body tracking, and a Wi-Fi 7 offloaded rendering path. The engine is being built to be driven by the best model available, whoever's model that is.

item.title: Meta Quest Is Now a Target
item.description: This week the runtime added OpenXR support for Meta Quest headsets running Horizon OS, including passthrough AR mode with composition layers and stereo rendering with 6DoF tracking. The engine now ships to one of the largest installed bases in spatial computing. Here is what that means and what is next.

item.title: Blade to Raku: Renaming a Runtime
item.description: This week the engine got a new name. The codebase renamed itself from blade and ar1-runtime to Raku. The rebrand surfaced a few things about how an agent-driven workflow handles a sweeping change.

item.title: Two Months In: What the Engine Has Become
item.description: Two months since the first commit. A quiet week. A good week to take stock of what the engine actually is now, where the architecture has settled, and what the road to a December production-readiness milestone looks like.

item.title: The Slow Week Was the Right Week
item.description: Thirty-seven commits this week instead of the eighty I have been used to. The agents were idle for two days. The repo did not break. Here is what I learned about pacing a workflow where the bottleneck is the human's attention, not the agent's throughput.

item.title: Sub-Millimeter Anchors and the Calligraphy Question
item.description: This week the runtime got a sub-millimeter anchor-pose API, real-time pen and brush tracking, and marker-based paper detection. The forcing function was a calligraphy tutor demo. The forcing function behind the forcing function was a partnership conversation with NTT QONOQ.

item.title: Validating Hardware We Don't Have Yet
item.description: This week the runtime pivoted from AR1+ to AR2 Gen1, and started shipping thermal-validation, Wi-Fi 7 stability tests, and a sensor-integration bring-up harness for hardware that does not exist on my desk. Here is why that is a sensible thing to do.

item.title: SDK Parity and the Two-Stream Bridge
item.description: This week the SDK caught up to the runtime. Unity and Unreal HelloAR samples both work. A parity-testing infrastructure proves the two bindings expose the same surface. The reason both repos moved in lockstep is the same reason agent-driven engineering produces a coherent codebase: discipline at the seams.

item.title: Week One: 282 Commits, One Founder, an Agent Queue
item.description: The first full week on the runtime: 282 commits across the repos, almost all of them landed by an autonomous coding agent pulling issues off a queue I refill every morning. Here is what got built, and what the workflow looks like when it works.

item.title: Day Zero: A README, a Spec, and a Bet on AR Glasses
item.description: The first commit was a README. Six days later we had a runtime skeleton, a smart-glasses spec, a CI pipeline, and a public commitment to building this thing with AI agents on the team from the start.
